He weeps, fasts, and prays, then asks permission to return and rebuild. The walls of Jerusalem are rebuilt in fifty-two days — against fierce opposition — by a community that works with tools in one hand and weapons in the other. Ezra the scribe then reads the Torah publicly in the square before the Water Gate, from morning to evening, as the people stand and weep. The Levites "gave the sense" — explaining and interpreting the law as it was read. This scene is the first recorded act of public biblical interpretation in history and the prototype of the homily.

Typological Connection The Nehemiah community — returned exiles rebuilding a broken city against opposition, sustained by reading the Word of God — is the prototype of every renewal in Church history. The pattern: community → opposition → prayer → Word of God → renewed fidelity. The Catholic principle of ressourcement (returning to the sources) follows the same logic: renewal comes not from innovation but from returning to the foundational texts and interpreting them faithfully for the present.
Traditional: 167–164 BCE
Historical-critical: 167–164 BCE — precisely confirmed; among the best-documented events in the OT period
The Maccabean Revolt: When the Torah Was Worth Dying For
1 Maccabees 1–4 · 2 Maccabees 7 · Daniel 11–12 · Hebrews 11:35–38

Antiochus IV Epiphanes, the Seleucid Greek king, determines to Hellenize Judea by force. He bans Torah observance, Sabbath, circumcision, and the dietary laws — making their practice punishable by death. He desecrates the Temple by sacrificing a pig on the altar and installing an image of Zeus. The "Abomination of Desolation" that Daniel predicts (Daniel 11:31) has arrived. The response is the Maccabean revolt — led by Judas Maccabeus ("the Hammer") and his brothers, a guerrilla campaign against the greatest military power of the age.

2 Maccabees 7 records the martyrdom of a mother and her seven sons, tortured and killed for refusing to eat pork. Their last words are among the most important in the entire OT: "You dismiss us from this present life, but the King of the universe will raise us up to an everlasting renewal of life, because we have died for his laws" (7:9). This is the clearest and most explicit statement of bodily resurrection in the OT — directly connected to martyrdom. The blood of the Maccabean martyrs gives birth to Jewish resurrection theology.

In December 164 BCE, the Maccabees recapture the Temple, cleanse it, and rededicate it — the event commemorated by Hanukkah (the Feast of Dedication). Jesus is in Jerusalem during Hanukkah (John 10:22–23) — the feast that celebrates the cleansing of the Temple that his own death and resurrection will definitively replace.

Typological Connection Hebrews 11:35–38 references the Maccabean martyrs: "Some were tortured, refusing to accept release, so that they might rise again to a better life... They were stoned, sawn in two, killed with the sword." The martyrs' cry — "The King of the universe will raise us up" — is fulfilled in the resurrection of Christ and, through him, all the faithful. The Maccabean period also produces the developed theology of angels, resurrection, and final judgment that forms the apocalyptic background of the NT. The prayer for the dead in 2 Maccabees 12:38–46 is the primary OT basis for the doctrine of purgatory.
Traditional: c. 450–400 BCE
Historical-critical: c. 450–400 BCE; Malachi is the last of the canonical prophets
Malachi: The Last Prophet — and the Silence
Malachi 3:1 · Malachi 4:5–6 · Matthew 11:14 · Luke 1:17

Malachi is the last book of the OT canon. His final words: "Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of the Lord comes. And he will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and the hearts of children to their fathers, lest I come and strike the land with a decree of utter destruction" (Malachi 4:5–6). After Malachi, the prophetic voice goes silent. Jewish tradition calls this period "the hiding of the face" — God is present but not speaking. The silence lasts approximately four centuries.

The period between Malachi and John the Baptist — roughly 400 BCE to 27 CE — is often called the "intertestamental period." It is not a dead period: it is filled with the development of the synagogue, the Septuagint translation, the Maccabean revolt, the Pharisee-Sadducee-Essene sectarian landscape, the writing of apocalyptic literature (Daniel, Enoch, 4 Ezra), the Roman conquest of Jerusalem (63 BCE, Pompey), and the reign of Herod the Great (37–4 BCE). All of this forms the context for the Incarnation.

Typological Connection When John the Baptist appears in the wilderness after 400 years of prophetic silence, the effect is seismic. "The word of God came to John son of Zechariah in the wilderness" (Luke 3:2) — the formula used for the great OT prophets. The silence has ended. And John is explicitly identified as Malachi's Elijah: "He will go before him in the spirit and power of Elijah" (Luke 1:17). The four-century silence is itself a type: the long preparation, the apparent absence of God, that precedes the definitive divine speech — the Word made flesh.
Era VIII — The Life, Death, and Resurrection of Jesus Christ Matthew · Mark · Luke · John · c. 6 BCE–30 CE
Traditional: c. 6 BCE (corrected Dionysian reckoning)
Historical-critical: c. 6–4 BCE: must predate Herod the Great's death (4 BCE). Dionysus Exiguus's 6th-century calendar calculation was off by approximately 6 years.
The Annunciation: God Enters Time
Luke 1:26–38 · Isaiah 7:14 · Genesis 3:15 · Galatians 4:4–5

The angel Gabriel appears to Mary in Nazareth — a young woman, probably 13–15 years old by the conventions of the time, betrothed to Joseph. The announcement is precise: she will conceive by the Holy Spirit and bear a son who will be called the Son of the Most High, who will receive the throne of his father David, whose kingdom will have no end. "Behold, I am the handmaid of the Lord," Mary says. "Let it be done to me according to your word" (Luke 1:38). With this act of consent, what God has been preparing since Creation — since the promise of Genesis 3:15, through every covenant and every type — begins its final act.

The Annunciation is the hinge of history. Paul's compressed description: "When the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of woman, born under the law" (Galatians 4:4). The phrase "fullness of time" (pleroma tou chronou) signals that the entire OT period has been a preparation — a gestation — for this moment. All the types converge here: the promised seed (Genesis 3:15), the son of Abraham (Galatians 3:16), the son of David (Luke 1:32), the Servant who will suffer (Isaiah 53), the Son of Man (Daniel 7:13). The Word does not merely appear; he is born of a woman, taking on the full weight of human history.

Mary's "fiat" — "let it be done to me" — is the reversal of Eve's grasping ("she took of its fruit and ate"). Eve acted in disobedience to a divine prohibition; Mary acts in obedience to a divine invitation. The Church Fathers (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus) developed this Eve-Mary typology from the 2nd century: "the knot of Eve's disobedience was loosed by Mary's obedience" (Irenaeus, Against Heresies III.22.4).

Typological Connection The Annunciation is the moment at which every OT type simultaneously finds its subject. Mary is the Ark of the New Covenant (carrying Christ as the Ark carried God's presence), the Daughter of Zion (the personified Israel receiving its fulfillment), the new Eve (whose obedience reverses the first disobedience), and the Temple (the Holy Spirit "overshadows" her — the same word used for the cloud that overshadowed the Tabernacle in Exodus 40:35). Her womb is the new Holy of Holies.
Traditional: c. 6–4 BCE, Bethlehem
Historical-critical: c. 6–4 BCE; the census of Quirinius is historically debated. Bethlehem as birthplace is multiply attested (Matthew and Luke independently). Herod's massacre of the infants is not attested in non-Christian sources but is consistent with his documented character.
The Nativity: God in a Manger
Luke 2:1–20 · Matthew 1:18–2:23 · Micah 5:2 · Isaiah 7:14 · Hosea 11:1

"And she gave birth to her firstborn son and wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger, because there was no place for them in the inn" (Luke 2:7). The Word by whom all things were made is laid in the stone feeding trough of Bethlehem's stable. Shepherds — the lowest of the low in social hierarchy, unable to observe ritual purity, their testimony inadmissible in court — are the first to receive the announcement. Angels appear, the sky fills with the Gloria, and the shepherds go in haste. The Magi from the East follow a star — Gentile astronomers drawn to the Jewish Messiah — and present gold, frankincense, and myrrh: gifts for a king, a priest, and a man who will die.

The two birth narratives (Matthew and Luke) are independent accounts that complement each other. Luke emphasizes the shepherds, the poverty, the Magnificat, the Presentation in the Temple. Matthew emphasizes the Magi, the flight to Egypt, the massacre of the innocents — situating Jesus within the Mosaic typology (the child who escapes a royal massacre, is brought out of Egypt, and delivers his people). Matthew cites Micah 5:2 (born in Bethlehem), Isaiah 7:14 (born of a virgin), Hosea 11:1 ("out of Egypt I called my son"), and Jeremiah 31:15 (Rachel weeping for her children) — four OT fulfillments in the nativity narrative alone.

Typological Connection The manger (a feeding trough) is where animals eat. Jesus is born in a feeding place and will say "Unless you eat my flesh and drink my blood you have no life in you" (John 6:53). The Fathers noted this: the eucharistic body of Christ is laid in the same place his physical body was laid — a feeding trough. The swaddling cloths in which Mary wraps him echo the burial cloths in which he will be wrapped after the crucifixion. Birth and death are already intertwined.
Traditional: c. 27–28 CE
Historical-critical: c. 27–29 CE; Luke 3:1–2 dates John's ministry to the 15th year of Tiberius (28–29 CE)
The Baptism and the Temptation: The Son Revealed and Tested
Matthew 3:13–4:11 · Mark 1:9–13 · Luke 3:21–4:13 · Isaiah 42:1 · Deuteronomy 8

Jesus comes to John at the Jordan River and is baptized — despite John's objection ("I need to be baptized by you"). As he comes up from the water, the heavens open, the Spirit descends like a dove, and the Father speaks: "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased" (Matthew 3:17). The voice echoes Psalm 2:7 ("You are my Son") and Isaiah 42:1 ("my chosen, in whom my soul delights") — simultaneously identifying Jesus as the royal Son (Davidic Messiah) and the Servant (Isaiah's suffering figure). The Baptism is the public manifestation of who Jesus is: the eternal Son in whom the Father is pleased, upon whom the Spirit rests permanently.

Immediately the Spirit drives Jesus into the wilderness — forty days of fasting and temptation, recapitulating Israel's forty years in the wilderness. Each of the devil's three temptations echoes an Israelite failure in the wilderness: turn stones to bread (cf. the demand for food at Meribah), throw yourself from the Temple to test God (cf. Massah, Exodus 17), worship me to receive the kingdoms (cf. the Golden Calf, the primal idolatry). Each time Jesus answers with a quotation from Deuteronomy — the book of Moses's address to the wilderness generation. Where Israel failed, Jesus succeeds. He is the true Israel, the obedient Son, completing what the first generation could not.

Typological Connection Jesus's forty days in the wilderness recapitulate Israel's forty years; his use of Deuteronomy to resist temptation shows him re-running the wilderness narrative with perfect fidelity. He is simultaneously the new Adam (resisting the devil's temptation that the first Adam yielded to), the new Israel (succeeding where the nation failed), and the new Moses (coming through the wilderness to lead his people into the true Promised Land). The Baptism inaugurates the new creation: dove (Spirit) over water (baptism) as in Genesis 1:2, Father's voice, new beginning.
Traditional: c. 28–30 CE
Historical-critical: c. 28–30 CE; the public ministry is generally placed between 1.5 and 3 years based on Synoptic and Johannine chronologies
The Sermon on the Mount: The New Law
Matthew 5–7 · Exodus 19–20 · Deuteronomy 5 · James 2:8

"Seeing the crowds, he went up on the mountain" (Matthew 5:1). Matthew's structural intention is unmistakable: the new Moses ascends the mountain to deliver the new law. The Beatitudes — blessings upon the poor in spirit, the mourning, the meek, the hungry and thirsty for righteousness, the merciful, the pure in heart, the peacemakers, the persecuted — invert the world's value system as comprehensively as the Magnificat. They do not describe how to become blessed; they describe who the blessed already are.

"You have heard that it was said... but I say to you." Six times in Matthew 5, Jesus contrasts the received interpretation of the law with his own authoritative restatement. He does not abolish the law — "I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them" (5:17) — but he deepens it, driving the prohibition on murder back to its root in anger, the prohibition on adultery back to its root in lust, the regulation of oaths back to the simple discipline of truthful speech. The Sermon on the Mount is not a new law that replaces the old but the old law read at its deepest possible level by the one who gave it.

Typological Connection The five great discourses of Matthew's Gospel (chapters 5–7, 10, 13, 18, 24–25) intentionally parallel the five books of Moses (the Pentateuch). Matthew presents Jesus as the new Moses — the authoritative teacher of Israel's law, fulfilling it from within. But Jesus's authority exceeds Moses's: Moses says "Thus says the Lord"; Jesus says "I say to you" — speaking in his own name with divine authority. The mountain, the law, the beatitudes as blessings (echoing Deuteronomy's blessings and curses) — all establish Jesus as the definitive interpreter and fulfillment of the Mosaic covenant.
Traditional: c. 29 CE
Historical-critical: Multiple attestation across all four Gospels makes the feeding of the multitude one of the most robustly documented events in Jesus's ministry
The Feeding of the Five Thousand: The Bread of Life
Matthew 14:13–21 · Mark 6:30–44 · Luke 9:10–17 · John 6:1–71 · Exodus 16

Five thousand men (plus women and children) follow Jesus into a deserted place. Late in the day, with no food for miles, the disciples want to send them away. Jesus says: "You give them something to eat." Five loaves and two fish — the disciples' offering, absurdly inadequate — are blessed, broken, distributed, and somehow feed everyone. Twelve baskets of fragments are collected: one for each tribe of Israel. The crowd wants to make him king (John 6:15). Jesus withdraws. The feeding is simultaneously a sign (of who Jesus is) and a misunderstood political gesture (the crowd wants a bread-king, not a crucified Messiah).

John 6 follows the feeding with the Bread of Life discourse — the most explicitly Eucharistic passage in the NT, set in the context of Passover: "I am the bread of life... the bread that I will give for the life of the world is my flesh" (6:35, 51). Many disciples leave at this point: "This is a hard saying; who can listen to it?" (6:60). Jesus does not soften it. He turns to the Twelve: "Do you also wish to go away?" Peter answers: "Lord, to whom would we go? You have the words of eternal life" (6:67–68).

Typological Connection The structure of the feeding — Jesus takes bread, gives thanks (eucharistesas), breaks it, and gives it to the disciples to distribute — is identical to the structure of the Last Supper institution narratives (Matthew 26:26–27) and the Emmaus meal (Luke 24:30). The feeding of the five thousand is a Eucharistic anticipation: a preview of what the Mass is. The manna in the wilderness fed Israel physically; the Eucharist feeds the Church with the living bread that came down from heaven (John 6:51). The twelve baskets of fragments mirror the twelve apostles who distribute the bread — the college through whom Christ feeds his Church.
Traditional: c. 29 CE
Historical-critical: Attested in all three Synoptics; the experience appears to be a genuine historical event, though its interpretation is theological
The Transfiguration: The Glory Revealed
Matthew 17:1–13 · Mark 9:2–13 · Luke 9:28–36 · 2 Peter 1:16–18 · Exodus 34:29–35

Six days after Peter's confession at Caesarea Philippi, Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain. "He was transfigured before them, and his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light" (Matthew 17:2). Moses and Elijah appear — representing the Law and the Prophets — and speak with him about his "departure" (Luke 9:31 — the Greek is exodon, his Exodus). A cloud overshadows them, the Father speaks: "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased; listen to him." The disciples fall on their faces. When they look up, they see only Jesus.

The Transfiguration is the single moment in the public ministry when the disciples see Jesus as he truly is — his divine glory visible through and in his human flesh. It is the preview of the Resurrection, given in advance so that the disciples will not be entirely destroyed by the scandal of the cross. It is also the OT fulfillment: Moses's face shone after he encountered God on Sinai (Exodus 34:29–35); Jesus shines from within because he is God incarnate, not a recipient of reflected light.

Typological Connection The "departure" (Exodus) that Moses and Elijah discuss with Jesus is the crucifixion-resurrection — the New Exodus by which Christ leads humanity out of the slavery of sin and death. Moses (who led the first Exodus) and Elijah (the prophet of Israel's greatest reform) both point toward and find their completion in Christ's definitive Exodus. 2 Peter 1:16–18 invokes the Transfiguration as the basis for the apostolic witness: "We were eyewitnesses of his majesty... we ourselves heard this very voice borne from heaven." The Transfiguration is the visual confirmation of the divine identity behind the human face.
Traditional: c. 30 or 33 CE, Passover week
Historical-critical: c. 30 or 33 CE; the Thursday-Friday sequence is well established. The date dispute (30 vs. 33 CE) turns on astronomical calculation of Passover dates and the year of John the Baptist's ministry beginning
The Last Supper: The New Covenant in His Blood
Matthew 26:26–29 · Mark 14:22–25 · Luke 22:14–20 · John 13–17 · 1 Corinthians 11:23–26 · Exodus 24:8 · Jeremiah 31:31–34

On the night before he dies, during or in connection with the Passover meal, Jesus takes bread, gives thanks, breaks it, and says: "Take, eat; this is my body." He takes a cup, gives thanks, and says: "Drink of it, all of you, for this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" (Matthew 26:26–28). He then says: "Do this in remembrance of me" — instituting a rite that is still performed two thousand years later at every Catholic Mass.

The phrase "my blood of the covenant" is a direct echo of Moses at Sinai: "This is the blood of the covenant" (Exodus 24:8). But it is also a fulfillment of Jeremiah 31:31: "I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel." Jesus is establishing the new covenant with his own blood — not the blood of animals, not the blood sprinkled on altar and people, but his own life given for the many. The Passover lamb is now the host at the Passover meal. The priest is also the sacrifice. The one who offers is also what is offered.

John's Gospel does not narrate the institution of the Eucharist at the Last Supper (it is given in John 6 instead) but devotes five chapters (13–17) to the Farewell Discourse: the washing of feet, the promise of the Paraclete (the Holy Spirit as advocate), the vine and the branches, the high-priestly prayer for unity ("that they may be one as we are one," 17:21). These chapters are the deepest theological meditation on the relationship between Christ and the Church in the entire NT.

Typological Connection The Last Supper fulfills every OT meal-covenant simultaneously. The Passover (Israel's redemption through the blood of a lamb) finds its antitype in the Lamb of God's blood. The covenant sacrifice at Sinai (blood on altar and people) finds its antitype in the blood poured out for the covenant. Melchizedek's offering of bread and wine (Genesis 14:18) — which has no prior context and no successor in the OT — finds its fulfillment in the Eucharist. Every Mass is, in the precise sense of the word, a Passover meal, a Sinai covenant-sealing, and a Melchizedekian priestly offering, all simultaneously.
Traditional: Friday, 14 Nisan, c. 30 or 33 CE
Historical-critical: The crucifixion under Pontius Pilate is among the most historically certain events in the life of Jesus, attested by Tacitus, Josephus, and all four Gospels
The Crucifixion: The Lamb of God
Matthew 27 · Mark 15 · Luke 23 · John 19 · Isaiah 53 · Psalm 22 · Zechariah 12:10

Jesus is arrested, tried by the Sanhedrin, handed to Pilate, flogged, crowned with thorns, and led to Golgotha ("the Place of the Skull") carrying his cross. He is crucified between two criminals at the third hour (9 AM) and dies at the ninth hour (3 PM) — the exact hour when the Passover lambs are being slaughtered in the Temple less than a mile away. His last words are the opening line of Psalm 22: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" — a psalm that proceeds from abandonment to trust, from desolation to praise, and ends with "Posterity will serve him; future generations will be told about the Lord. They will proclaim his deliverance to a people yet unborn — that he has done it."

The physical details of the crucifixion fulfill OT prophecies with a precision that either suggests divine design or astonishing retrospective literary invention: the casting of lots for his garments (Psalm 22:18), the vinegar offered (Psalm 69:21), no bone broken (Exodus 12:46/Psalm 34:20), pierced side (Zechariah 12:10). The Temple curtain is torn from top to bottom (Matthew 27:51) — the barrier separating the Holy of Holies from the people, representing the separation between God and humanity, is permanently removed. "And the earth shook, and the rocks were split. The tombs also were opened" (27:51–52) — creation convulses at the death of its Creator.

The Roman centurion, a Gentile, is the first person to confess Christ at the cross: "Truly this man was the Son of God" (Mark 15:39). The first Christian theologian is a pagan soldier. The Gospel reaches the Gentiles at the moment of its founding.

Typological Connection Every major OT type converges at Golgotha. The Passover Lamb (his blood shed for the liberation of God's people at the exact hour of the Passover sacrifice). The Akedah (the beloved Son, the wood, the mountain, but this time God provides no ram). The Servant of Isaiah 53 ("pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities"). The bronze serpent (lifted up on the pole for those who look in faith). The scapegoat (bearing the sins of the people outside the camp — Hebrews 13:12). The high priest entering the Holy of Holies with blood (Hebrews 9) — but in this case the priest is the sacrifice. The cross is the altar, the priest, and the sacrifice simultaneously.
Traditional: Sunday, 16 Nisan, c. 30 or 33 CE
Historical-critical: The disciples' resurrection belief is historically certain; the empty tomb is multiply attested; historian N.T. Wright argues the bodily resurrection is the best historical explanation for the emergence of Christianity
The Resurrection: The Eighth Day
Matthew 28 · Mark 16 · Luke 24 · John 20–21 · 1 Corinthians 15:3–8 · Romans 1:4 · Acts 2:24

"He is not here. He has risen, as he said" (Matthew 28:6). On the first day of the week — the day after the Sabbath, the eighth day, the day of new creation — the tomb is empty and Jesus appears alive. He appears to Mary Magdalene, to Peter, to the two on the road to Emmaus (who recognize him in the breaking of bread), to the Eleven (who think they are seeing a ghost until he eats broiled fish), and to more than five hundred at once (1 Corinthians 15:6). The appearances are deliberate and varied: in the garden, in the upper room, by the sea of Tiberias, on the road, on a mountain.

The Resurrection is not the resuscitation of a corpse — Lazarus was resuscitated, and he died again. The Resurrection is a transformation: the body is continuous with what died ("Touch me and see; a ghost does not have flesh and bones as you see I have," Luke 24:39) but also discontinuous — he appears and disappears, passes through locked doors, is sometimes not immediately recognized. He is the firstfruit of the general resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20) — the first human being to have undergone the transformation that awaits all the dead at the end of time. The Resurrection is not an escape from matter but matter's glorification.

Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15 is the most systematic treatment: "If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins." The Resurrection is not a spiritual metaphor for new life or a symbol of spring. It is the event on which everything stands or falls. Without it, the entire structure collapses. With it, "death has been swallowed up in victory" (1 Corinthians 15:54).

Typological Connection "Eighth day" theology: Jewish tradition counted to seven (the Sabbath), the fullness of creation. The eighth day transcends the week — it is the beginning of a new order. Sunday (the day of Resurrection) is the eighth day — beyond the Sabbath, the first day of new creation. Early Christian baptism was performed on the eighth day. Church buildings were traditionally octagonal. The number eight in Christian symbolism is always the Resurrection: the new creation breaking into the old. The dry bones of Ezekiel 37 that breathe again, the three-day Jonas rising from the whale, Isaac "received back" after three days — all types of the eighth-day Resurrection.
Era IX — The Apostolic Age Acts · Epistles of Paul · Catholic Epistles · c. 30–100 CE
Traditional: c. 30 CE, fifty days after Passover
Historical-critical: c. 30 CE; the rapid growth of the Jerusalem church described in Acts is historically plausible given the conditions of 1st-century Judaism
Pentecost: The Church Is Born
Acts 2 · Joel 2:28–32 · Jeremiah 31:33 · Ezekiel 36:26–27 · Genesis 11 (Babel)

Fifty days after Passover — the feast of Shavuot (Weeks), which Jewish tradition associated with the giving of the Torah at Sinai — the apostles and 120 disciples are gathered in Jerusalem. "Suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the entire house where they were sitting. And divided tongues as of fire appeared to them and rested on each one of them. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues" (Acts 2:2–4).

Jews from "every nation under heaven" — the diaspora assembled for the feast — each hear the apostles in their own language. Peter stands up and preaches the first Christian sermon, quoting Joel 2:28–32 ("In the last days... I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh") and Psalm 16 and 110, explaining that Jesus who was crucified is both Lord and Christ. Three thousand are baptized in a single day. The Church is born.

The timing is precise and deliberate: Pentecost falls fifty days after Passover, as Sinai fell fifty days after the Exodus. The new law (the Holy Spirit) is given on the anniversary of the old law (the Torah). Jeremiah's new covenant promise — "I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts" (31:33) — is fulfilled: the Spirit inscribes the law of love not on stone tablets but on human hearts, from within.

Typological Connection Pentecost is the consummation of every prophetic promise in the OT about the Spirit: Isaiah's promise of the Spirit's outpouring (Isaiah 44:3), Ezekiel's new heart and new spirit (36:26–27), Joel's universal outpouring on all flesh (2:28–32), and Jeremiah's new covenant law written on hearts (31:33). All five converge at the upper room. And Pentecost is the anti-Babel: at Babel, one language became many and humanity was scattered; at Pentecost, many languages converge in understanding and the scattered nations are gathered into the Church.
Traditional: c. 34–36 CE
Historical-critical: c. 33–36 CE; Paul himself describes his conversion in Galatians 1:13–17, placing it shortly after the crucifixion
The Conversion of Paul: The Apostle to the Gentiles
Acts 9:1–19 · Galatians 1:13–17 · 1 Corinthians 15:9–10 · Philippians 3:4–11

Saul of Tarsus — a Pharisee of the Pharisees, a persecutor of the Church, trained under Gamaliel — is traveling to Damascus to arrest Christians when a blinding light surrounds him and he falls to the ground. "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?" — "Who are you, Lord?" — "I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting." The identification is decisive: the risen Christ is identified with the community he claims, so that to persecute Christians is to persecute Christ himself. Saul is blind for three days, then healed and baptized by Ananias.

Paul becomes the most important missionary figure in the history of Christianity — the apostle who carries the Gospel to Asia Minor, Greece, and Rome; who writes the NT's most theologically sophisticated letters (Romans, Galatians, 1–2 Corinthians, Philippians, Ephesians, Colossians); who articulates the doctrine of justification by faith, the theology of the Body of Christ, and the cosmic scope of Christ's lordship. He is eventually executed in Rome under Nero, c. 64–67 CE.

Typological Connection Paul's conversion is itself a type of what the Gospel does to every person: the persecutor becomes the witness, the enemy becomes the ambassador, the one who scattered the community becomes the one who gathers it. Paul explicitly describes himself as "the worst" sinner (1 Timothy 1:15) saved as "an example" — so that no one could claim the Gospel's grace is insufficient for their own case. His conversion is also the fulfillment of Isaiah's promise that the nations will be reached through Israel's suffering servant: Paul is the instrument through whom the servant's suffering reaches the Gentile world.
Traditional: c. 49 CE
Historical-critical: c. 48–50 CE; Galatians 2 provides Paul's own account of the meeting
The Council of Jerusalem: Gentiles Are In
Acts 15 · Galatians 2 · Ephesians 2:11–22 · Amos 9:11–12

The question threatening to split the early Church: must Gentile converts be circumcised and observe the Mosaic law to be saved? The Council of Jerusalem (Acts 15) — the first council in Church history — deliberates, hears testimony from Peter, Paul, and Barnabas, and James delivers the judgment: Gentiles need not be circumcised. He quotes Amos 9:11–12 as the OT basis: God is "rebuilding the tent of David" so that "all the Gentiles who are called by my name" might seek the Lord. The conclusion: "We should not trouble those of the Gentiles who turn to God."

This decision, reached collegially by the apostolic community under the leadership of James (the bishop of Jerusalem), is the prototype of all subsequent councils. It also definitively opens the covenant to the Gentile world — what Babel scattered, what Abraham was promised ("in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed"), and what every prophetic hope for the nations points toward is now formally received into the Church's practice.

Typological Connection Paul's letter to the Ephesians describes the theological meaning of what the Jerusalem Council decided: "He has made both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility... that he might create in himself one new man in place of the two, so making peace" (Ephesians 2:14–15). The wall in the Jerusalem Temple that separated the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts — with a warning inscription threatening death to any Gentile who crossed it — is abolished in Christ. The one new humanity, Jew and Gentile in one Body, is the fulfillment of Genesis 12:3 ("in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed").
Traditional: 70 CE
Historical-critical: 70 CE — extremely well attested by Josephus's detailed account in The Jewish War; confirmed by the Arch of Titus in Rome, which depicts the Temple vessels being carried in triumph
The Destruction of the Temple: The End of the Old Order
Matthew 24:1–2 · Luke 21:20–24 · Daniel 9:26–27 · John 4:21–24 · Hebrews 10

Forty years after the crucifixion, Titus's Roman legions besiege Jerusalem, breach the walls, slaughter the population, and burn the Temple to the ground. Jesus had predicted it precisely: "Do you see these great buildings? There will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down" (Mark 13:2). The Temple fire is so intense that the gold ornamentation melts and flows between the stones — to extract it, the soldiers pry apart every stone. Not one stone is left upon another.

For the Church, the destruction of the Temple confirms the NT's theology: the Temple as the place of sacrifice has been superseded by the one, definitive sacrifice of Christ (Hebrews 10:11–14: "every priest stands daily at his service, offering repeatedly the same sacrifices... but when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God"). The Levitical sacrificial system, which required a standing Temple, ends in 70 CE — forty years after the one sacrifice that made it obsolete. The type is removed when the reality has arrived.

Typological Connection The forty years between the crucifixion and the Temple's destruction echo the forty years of wilderness wandering between the Exodus and the entry into Canaan. In both cases, forty years separates the founding event from the closing of the old order. The Temple's destruction is not the defeat of the Christian God but the confirmation of Christ's claim: "I will destroy this temple made with hands, and in three days I will build another, not made with hands" (Mark 14:58). His body, the new Temple, already stands — and from it flows the water of eternal life (John 19:34, 7:38–39; Ezekiel 47:1–12).
Era X — The New Creation: Revelation and the Promised End Revelation · c. 90–96 CE and beyond
Traditional: c. 90–96 CE (John on Patmos, during Domitian's persecution)
Historical-critical: c. 90–96 CE is the scholarly consensus; a minority view dates it to c. 68 CE (Nero's persecution). The internal evidence for Domitian's reign is stronger.
The Book of Revelation: The Lamb Who Was Slain
Revelation 1 · Revelation 5 · Revelation 7 · Revelation 12 · Revelation 21–22 · Daniel 7 · Isaiah 65:17 · Ezekiel 40–48

John — exiled on the island of Patmos during Domitian's persecution — receives a vision of the risen Christ, "one like a son of man, clothed with a long robe and with a golden sash around his chest. The hairs of his head were white, like white wool, like snow. His eyes were like a flame of fire... his face was like the sun shining in full strength" (Revelation 1:13–16). This is the resurrected, glorified Christ — no longer the itinerant rabbi of the Gospels but the cosmic Lord of history.

The vision unfolds in elaborate symbolic imagery saturated with OT allusion. The throne room scene (Revelation 4–5) is a heavenly liturgy: four living creatures (echoing Ezekiel's vision), twenty-four elders, seven lamps (the Menorah), the sea of glass. A scroll sealed with seven seals cannot be opened by anyone — until the Lamb appears, "standing as though it had been slain" (5:6). The Lamb alone is worthy: he has ransomed people "from every tribe and language and people and nation" (5:9). All creation erupts in worship: "Worthy is the Lamb who was slain."

The vision climaxes in Revelation 21–22: a new heaven and a new earth, the New Jerusalem descending from heaven like a bride adorned for her husband, God dwelling with his people ("the tabernacle of God is with man"), no more death or mourning or crying or pain. The river of the water of life flows from the throne of God and of the Lamb; the tree of life (last seen in Genesis 3, guarded by cherubim) stands on both sides of the river. The curse of Genesis 3 is lifted: "No longer will there be anything accursed." The serpent who entered the first garden has been cast into the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10). The story that began with a garden ends in a city.

Typological Connection — The Whole Bible in Summary Revelation is the Bible reading itself. Every image in it comes from the OT: the four living creatures (Ezekiel), the sealed scroll (Ezekiel 2), the Lamb (Exodus and Isaiah), the New Jerusalem (Ezekiel 40–48 and Isaiah 60–62), the river of life (Ezekiel 47), the tree of life (Genesis 2–3), the new heaven and new earth (Isaiah 65:17). The beginning of Genesis and the end of Revelation form a perfect inclusio: garden → city; tree of life (guarded, inaccessible) → tree of life (restored, freely accessible); God walking with humanity in the evening → God dwelling permanently with humanity forever; the serpent enters → the serpent is cast out; "you will die" → "death shall be no more." The story God began at creation is complete. The exile is over. The Lamb is on the throne. Come, Lord Jesus.
